evals.expected_output: Extracts all user-facing strings (heading, subtitle, placeholders, link text, button, sign up link), generates dot-notation keys scoped to LoginForm, shows before/after code diff with t() calls, outputs keys for en.json. Skips className string. Flags the dynamic error string.

assertions.text: Extracts 'Welcome back' and generates a key like LoginForm.title or auth.login.title
Extracts both placeholder strings (Email address, Password)
Extracts 'Forgot your password?' link text
Extracts 'Log in' button text
Does NOT extract 'error' className string
Shows before/after code diff with t() or useTranslations wrapping
Outputs a JSON block with all new keys for en.json

evals.expected_output: Coverage report showing es is missing: common.loading, common.error, auth.login.forgot_password, dashboard.items_count. Also flags placeholder mismatch: en uses {name} but es uses {nombre}. Shows coverage percentage.

assertions.text: Reports missing keys: common.loading, common.error, auth.login.forgot_password, dashboard.items_count
Flags placeholder mismatch between {name} (en) and {nombre} (es)
Shows a coverage percentage for Spanish
Structures output clearly with missing keys listed

evals.prompt: I need to add i18n to my Vue 3 app. It currently has no i18n setup at all. Here's a sample component: ```vue ``` We want to support English and Japanese.
evals.expected_output: Recommends vue-i18n, provides install and setup instructions, shows the component rewritten with $t() calls, provides initial en.json and ja.json (Japanese values left as TODO or empty), generates appropriate keys

assertions.text: Recommends vue-i18n as the library
Provides install command (npm install vue-i18n)
Shows component rewritten using $t() or useI18n
Generates en.json with all extracted strings
Mentions Japanese locale file (ja.json) with empty or TODO values — does NOT invent Japanese translations
Mentions Japanese has no plural forms (nplurals=1)

evals.expected_output: Flags welcome, payment_success, password_reset as user-facing strings needing i18n. Correctly skips the log string, cssClass, and apiEndpoint. Also flags the hardcoded $ currency symbol as a localization issue — should use Intl.NumberFormat. Warns about dynamic key pattern messages[type].

assertions.text: Flags 'Welcome to our platform!' as needing i18n
Flags payment_success message as needing i18n
Flags hardcoded $ currency symbol as a localization issue
Does NOT flag the internal log string, cssClass, or apiEndpoint
Warns about dynamic key lookup (messages[type]) being undetectable by static analysis

evals.prompt:
I have these keys in my en.json. Are the key names good? How would you improve them?

```json
{
  "text1": "Save",
  "button": "Cancel",
  "Save Changes": "Save Changes",
  "en_welcome": "Welcome",
  "err": "An error occurred",
  "usrNm": "Username"
}
```
evals.expected_output: Identifies all anti-patterns: text1/button are non-descriptive, 'Save Changes' uses value as key, en_welcome hard-codes language, err/usrNm are cryptic abbreviations. Proposes improved keys like common.save, common.cancel, auth.login.title, errors.generic, auth.form.username_label

assertions.text: Flags 'text1' and 'button' as non-descriptive keys
Flags 'Save Changes' as using the string value as the key
Flags 'en_welcome' for hardcoding language in key name
Flags 'err' and 'usrNm' as cryptic abbreviations
Proposes improved namespaced key names for all 6 keys
